title: PDF attachments are corrupted after upload
body: When customers upload PDF files to their tasks, the files come through broken. You can't open them — the PDF viewer says the file is damaged. This is happening for multiple customers. We use the file attachment feature heavily so this is really impacting our workflow.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — corrupted file uploads are definitely something we want to get to the bottom of quickly.

Could you walk me through the exact steps to reproduce this? For example:
- How are you uploading the PDF? (drag-and-drop, the attach button, API, etc.)
- What size are the PDFs roughly, and are they all affected or only certain ones?
- When you download the file back, how are you opening it, and what exact error does the PDF viewer show?

This will help us pinpoint where the corruption is happening.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: We're using the attach button in the task view, same way we always have. I don't know the exact sizes — they're just normal PDFs, reports and contracts and stuff. Some seem to work fine and some don't, I haven't figured out a pattern. When you try to open the downloaded file, Adobe just says "There was an error opening this document. The file is damaged and could not be repaired." Look, this was working fine until recently. Something changed on your end — can you just check what was updated in the last week or two?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for those details — the Adobe error message and the upload steps are really helpful.

Could you let us know what environment you're working in? Specifically:
- **Browser and version** (e.g., Chrome 124, Firefox 126)
- **Operating system** (e.g., Windows 11, macOS Sonoma)
- **TaskFlow version** if you know it (visible in Settings → About)

This will help us narrow down whether the corruption is browser-specific or something on the server side.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Chrome, latest version, I don't know the exact number. Windows 10. I think TaskFlow says 2.3.1 in the settings. But honestly I don't think this is a browser thing — multiple people on my team are hitting this and they're not all on the same setup. Something changed on your end recently, that's what you should be looking at.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"PDF attachments corrupted on download — intermittent, recent regression (v2.3.1)","problem":"PDF files uploaded via the attach button in task view are sometimes corrupted when downloaded. Adobe Acrobat reports 'The file is damaged and could not be repaired.' The issue is intermittent — some PDFs upload and download fine, others do not. Multiple users across different browser/OS setups are affected, indicating a server-side issue. The reporter states this is a recent regression; uploads were working correctly until approximately 1-2 weeks ago.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A recent server-side change (within the last 1-2 weeks) is corrupting certain PDF uploads or downloads. Likely candidates: (1) a change in the file upload pipeline that incorrectly processes binary data for some files (e.g., encoding conversion, chunking, or content-type handling), (2) a storage backend change that truncates or modifies files above a certain size or with certain characteristics, or (3) a CDN/proxy layer change that interferes with binary content delivery.","reproduction_steps":["Open a task in TaskFlow 2.3.1","Click the attach button in the task view","Upload a PDF file (try several — the issue is intermittent)","Download the uploaded PDF","Attempt to open the downloaded file in Adobe Acrobat","Observe error: 'The file is damaged and could not be repaired'"],"environment":"TaskFlow 2.3.1, Chrome (latest) on Windows 10. Reproduced by multiple users across different browser/OS configurations.","severity":"high","impact":"Multiple customers are affected. Teams that rely heavily on PDF attachments (reports, contracts) are unable to use the file attachment feature reliably, disrupting core workflows.","recommended_fix":"1. Review all server-side changes to the file upload/download pipeline in the last two weeks. 2. Compare byte-for-byte a corrupted downloaded PDF against its original to identify where corruption occurs (truncation, encoding, byte substitution). 3. Check whether corruption happens at upload (stored file is already bad) or download (stored file is fine but served incorrectly) by examining files directly in the storage backend. 4. Test with PDFs of varying sizes and characteristics to identify the triggering pattern.","proposed_test_case":"Upload a set of diverse PDF files (varying sizes, single-page vs multi-page, text-only vs image-heavy) via the attach button, download each one, and verify the downloaded file matches the original byte-for-byte (SHA-256 comparison). This test should run against both the current version and the last known-good deployment to confirm the regression.","information_gaps":["Exact characteristics that distinguish corrupted PDFs from ones that upload successfully (file size, PDF version, content type)","Precise date the issue started occurring","Whether the stored files in the backend are already corrupted or only corrupted on download"]}
